comment_text.sn12.3:1.2: This discourse, located so prominently in the Saṁyutta, heads off the misconception that dependent origination is purely a theoretical or philosophical doctrine. Obviously it has a philosophical dimension, but it is aligned with the path of practice which also proceeds via cessation. The next Sutta shows how the sequence unfolded as a contemplative reflection.

root_text.sn12.3:1.3: Taṁ suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn12.3:1.4: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.

translation_text.sn12.3:1.2: “Mendicants, I will teach you the wrong practice and the right practice.
translation_text.sn12.3:1.3: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn12.3:2.2: Ignorance is a requirement for choices.
translation_text.sn12.3:2.3: Choices are a requirement for consciousness. …
translation_text.sn12.3:2.4: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.3:2.5: This is called the wrong practice.
translation_text.sn12.3:3.2: When ignorance fades away and ceases with nothing left over, choices cease.
translation_text.sn12.3:3.4: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.
translation_text.sn12.3:3.5: This is called the right practice.”
